speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number eight, an interview with Regina Mauskopf. How long did you stay in Brooklyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: We in '51, we came to Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Washington?

speaker: interviewee
text: Washington in the fall of '51.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have lived in Washington--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we lived in Washington. We lived in Taylor Street. And my husband was working as a delivery man at a grocery store, for his two brothers-- that one, because the one in Brooklyn who was living, he moved here because here was another of-- a real brother of his. They are kind of like by marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your work--

speaker: interviewee
text: But we never knew that they are-- not, we knew, but never had that feeling. I mean, you know, it's brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked with him in the grocery store?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did-- I did-- well, I had not that time, because the first one, the older one, retired. He went out of the store. So then, when my husband took over half of the share. And then in a couple years later, the other one got sick. And he had to take-- I mean he sold it for a partner. And then the partner wanted to go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then I went in. Our Roslyn was four years old, that time. So I went in with my husband, I mean, up and down.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived upstairs. We had a very nice apartment upstairs. And I've been working here, kind of in the middle. Went upstairs, send the children into school. And I went down, and then, after for [INAUDIBLE] the children came home, running up and running down. And Roslyn was a child. If it was the nice, she was on the tricycle outside. If not, she was upstairs. And she learned to dial the phone, and kept on dialing every minute. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you mention children. Can you identify your children? Who are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Roslyn Mauskopf and Norman Mauskopf.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Roslyn was born in '56-- BARRY (HUSBAND): '58. No, Barry. Please. [SQUEAL]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, it's no-- Roslyn-- Isn't that funny? She was born in '56, '57. And Norman was born in '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does Roslyn do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she finished law school. And she was an assistant district attorney in New York for Morgenthau. And then she was there for the fraud division in New York, too. And after the governor from New York got elected, and he needed a general inspector.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's what she is?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what she-- she was talking to Morgenthau about it. They had lunch lots of times, [INAUDIBLE] a very kind of nice thing. So she asked him on advice, what to do. And he told her that if you like it, you take that. But he told her that's going to be a very responsible job.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Norman, what does he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Norman, he's a freelance photographer. He is, by profession, an economist. He graduated from American University, and went to Brookings Institute working. And then they put him to the Federal Reserve. But he was unhappy to be on inside the work in an office. So he went to art school on Pasadena photography. And he got a scholarship after a semester. And then he was teaching there, in the school. And in UCLA, he had two classes that he-- And then he moved to Santa Fe. He lives now there. And he has his work. He travels a lot. He has three books published. I mean, two published. The third is coming out this-- I don't know when it's going to be-- if it's going to be before New Year's, or after New Year's. It's photography books kind of, the third one is-- I really didn't grasp, yet, what kind of the title is of things, because things. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me this, Mrs. Mauskopf. When you think back, your experiences in the Holocaust-- which is quite extensive and horrific to say the least-- what-- what are your thoughts today? What is it that you would like your children to-- to come away with, in terms of your experience, as it relates to the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] In-- in which way?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, do you have any thoughts at all about the Holocaust as you experienced it? What is the lesson for you, or for anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's scary. Well, I'll tell you something about another thing, about a thing. When we came here to Washington, the children were small. In that time, was the Nazi party-- Rockwell? Rockwell was his name. And here they killed him. I didn't know-- until then, I didn't know that there is such things as a Nazi, here in the United States. You know, I wasn't bothering with politics, and with things, you know. But when I saw him in the papers, or on the television, or they start talking, you know, that-- Rockwell was his name, I think. And that there is a Nazi party, you know. And it was, somewhere-- it was kind of in Virginia, very kind of going on at that time.

words.text: I didn't know that there is

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I I even said-- I don't know if I said it for my husband or not. But I did said, probably for brother-in-law, sister-in-law, if it's going to come again, something like that, I was very kind of-- had that lump in my stomach start working even more. And if that-- I'm not going to let my children going through. I'll do something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for a mother to say-- to think-- of to do something with the children, they should not live through something like that. That I am ashamed as a mother, that I had thoughts like that, that time. Because I was-- to me, it was very scary. I had two German friends when we're there and everything, the next door, at the beauty parlor. And they were German. And I went there. I have a friend there that's in the German embassy, Herman-- when we go to, you know the German embassy we hug each other. He says he comes out from that little window, what he signs there-- you know. He is not there now. It's a different kind of arrangement there for the Holocaust people. But with a wife, you know, the children there, you know, when we went in the store. Because they lived right there. And they had the beauty parlor there, right next door. And we were friends kind of. It's inside. No question about it. But you can just-- how shall I say-- carrying hate. You could carry fear. But hate, you can go on like with it. So [SIGHS] I really had one Siegfried. He looks like Hitler, him. And he used to come in the store, and doing that achtung with his feet. He used to sing, sometimes, that 'Deutschland Uber Alles.' And he was such a mean guy. I mean, we tried, and we-- we kind of-- him we separated, because at one time I got scared of him, the way he start talking.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's what it is. And the rest of it is, as I say, at that time, that's what I was thinking. Now, well, for the Yidden, I'm scared for Israel, terribly. I'm scared for the United States-- would you believe it? Because still, the United States is standing. I think that's how long the Yidden are going to stand. I want to thank you so much for this opportunity to interview you on behalf of the Shoah Foundation. Thank you again, so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I want to thank you, because you suggested that to my husband, if I want to do it. And I was just waiting for something. You know, I just didn't know how to start about it, because I'm so glad. Now, as I say, my time is kind of running out. And I'm glad that I have this done, that my family and whoever-- the perishables I call them; not just my family, but everybody in the world-- the perishables are not forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my husband, Barry. And this is our son, Norman. I wish our daughter would be here, but that's how life is. And that's it. How about, Barry?

speaker: interviewee
text: BARRY (HUSBAND): This is my wife. She is not just my wife, she is my best friend. Without her, I would never make it. She is the one that-- that's it. NORMAN (SON): I'd like to say, on behalf of my sister Roslynn and I, that we are as proud of Mom and Dad as they are of us. Thank you very much. BARRY (HUSBAND): Thank you. This is my sister [? Meryl ?] with her husband. And they lived in Holland. The picture was taken something between '29 or '31.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Holland. This is my mother. The picture was taken in front yard of my two nieces-- which, we've been together in the camps. And it was taken around '33, '34. 1933, '34. This is my brother [NON-ENGLISH] and his wife Hannah. And this was when they were engaged. It was approximately in-- in '35. This is my brother [NON-ENGLISH] and his son Jacob. And he was taking him-- he was emigrating. The son was emigrating to the United States to study. And he went with him to Prague. And there where the picture was taken. And it was something like maybe-- he was in 1931 or '32. [SIGHS] Well, this is a happy time picture, already, at home after the liberation, when we went. This is in my brother's back yard, when after we came home. And it was taken in the middle of August of 1945, when we was liberated. And there is the little one, the daughter, my niece, and then me. And then in the back is another survivor from our village. And then is [? Laya, ?] my niece. And then is my cousins-- my sister-in-law's cousin. And then, the short one in the scarf is what she was with me and my cousin, daughter in the camps. And in the front there two sitting, again, they are survivors. This is my sister, [? Frida. ?] And that picture was taken in the '20s, somewhere-- between '20, '25, something like that. Maybe '28. There is my sister [? Malka ?] with her husband, there. And that was taken in '35, '36. And there is a group of friends with them. This is my brother, [NON-ENGLISH]. And that picture was taken between '25 and '30 in Prague. That's my sister-in-law, one-- the wife of one of [NON-ENGLISH] brother. And she holds a little boy, Benjamin. And it was taken, too, in '37, something, in the front of the-- I mean, in the back yard. Somewhere there is their house. That's my brother's house in the next village. He was living in [NON-ENGLISH]. And there is with his wife and four children. And there is my brother. He took the picture, the one which [INAUDIBLE]. And another friend behind. The boy in the front is an onlooker. That's myself in-- after the liberation in 1946. And it was taken in Bergen-Belsen [INAUDIBLE]. That's it. I don't know exactly which [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] place. This is a letter from Russia, which after the liberation, we got separated. They remained there, in the [? Carpathian. ?] And when we finally got contact with them, she just wrote to me and thanked for what we were sending packages to them. And she wrote to me that if wouldn't be for me, they would die in the camps, for hunger. This is the camp Plaszow. I tried to, the way I saw it-- the middle, especially. The sides, I don't know what-- it was still more there. And that's what I saw it. I mean, I remember it-- the barracks and the places I worked. And it was in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 1944, in the middle of-- between the 15th and in August. This picture was taken in June, 1996. Our daughter had a press conference there, with Governor Pataki. And there was I, and my husband, Barry, and our daughter, and the Governor Pataki.
